mnemonics.title: Sustainability Pillars记忆
mnemonics.content: PES - People, Environment, Society. Think: People need Environment for Society to thrive.

mnemonics.title: Green IT Challenges记忆
mnemonics.content: E3S - Energy, E-waste, Economic, Social. The '3' stands for three main areas plus 'Social' makes it E³S.

mnemonics.title: PUE记忆
mnemonics.content: Perfect Utilization Equals 1.0. As PUE goes up, efficiency goes down. Remember: PUE > 1 means waste!

acronyms.usage: Measures data center energy efficiency. Lower is better.
Centralized desktop management reducing energy consumption.
EU directive for proper e-waste disposal and recycling.
EU directive limiting toxic materials in electronic products.
Discarded electronic devices and equipment requiring special disposal.
EU regulation affecting data center location and energy use.
Standards for sustainability reporting in organizations.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pie chart: IT equipment is the inner circle, total facility is the outer circle. PUE = outer/inner. The smaller the difference between circles, the closer to 1.0 PUE.
Think of a three-legged stool: Environmental (ground/roots), Economic (seat), Social (person sitting). All three legs must be equal for the stool to stand.
Think of a reverse waterfall: Products flow DOWN from manufacturing to consumers, then e-waste flows UP to developing countries causing pollution.
Imagine one big house (physical server) with many small apartments (virtual machines) instead of many small houses (individual servers). Much more efficient use of space and resources!

analogies.analogy: Like a light bulb designed to burn out after 1000 hours instead of lasting forever - it's built to need replacement.
Like a factory that needs power for both manufacturing (IT equipment) AND climate control (cooling). A poorly designed factory uses as much energy for cooling as for production!
It's like choosing between a cheap SUV that guzzles gas vs. a pricier hybrid that saves money long-term - initial cost vs. lifetime savings.
Like a food calorie count but for environmental impact - each IT activity 'burns' a certain amount of environmental 'energy' measured in carbon emissions.
